title: $5 Cedar Bird Feeder
summary: A gabled open-platform bird feeder from one cedar fence picket — six cuts, glue, and brads. The beginner project that ends up in everyone's yard by spring.
description:
One dog-eared cedar fence picket, about five dollars, becomes a proper gabled bird feeder: two peaked end walls, an offset two-piece roof, a floor, and side rails that keep the seed from blowing off the platform. It's the ideal first project with a brad nailer — every joint is glue plus pins, and nothing is bigger than your hand.

The design quietly handles fence-picket reality. Pickets run nominally 1/2" thick but vary, so two of the cuts are measure-to-fit: the side rails are cut to the assembled feeder's actual length, and one roof panel gets ripped 1/2" narrower than the other so the two overlap into an even peak. Cut the fixed dimensions first, assemble, then fit the variable pieces to what you actually built.

Use a glue rated for oily woods — cedar's natural oils starve ordinary glue joints. An eye hook at the roof peak hangs it; a lag from below mounts it on a post. Either way, put it where you can see it from a window, because that's the entire return on investment.

tools.note: The 45° gable cuts. A circular saw with marked lines works.
The rip that narrows the shorter roof panel.
Pilot the eye hook at the peak if hanging.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72"
materials.note: Dog-eared is fine — the eared end becomes offcut. Nominal 1/2" thick; the plan absorbs the variation.

materials.name: Exterior wood glue, rated for oily woods
materials.note: Cedar's oils defeat ordinary glue — the rating matters.

materials.name: Eye hook
materials.note: For hanging from the roof peak.

cutList.note: Top cut to a center peak with 45° angles both sides.
Rip 1/2" off one edge — the narrower panel tucks under the wider one at the peak.
Measure and cut to fit your assembled feeder, then rip into 1-1/2" strips.

steps.title: Attach the gable walls to the floor
steps.body: Mark the peak on each wall piece with the speed square — 45° from each side to a center point — and cut. Then glue and brad both walls to the ends of the floor, square and flush at the edges.
materials: Exterior wood glue, rated for oily woods

steps.title: Attach the narrow roof panel
steps.body: Rip the shorter roof panel to 5" wide, then glue and nail it to one slope of the gables, its top edge landing at the peak line.
materials: Exterior wood glue, rated for oily woods

steps.title: Cap with the wide roof panel
steps.body: Glue and nail the full-width panel to the other slope so it overlaps the narrow panel's top edge — that overlap IS the ridge, and it sheds rain away from the seed. Nail into both the gables and the narrow panel's edge.
materials: Exterior wood glue, rated for oily woods

steps.title: Fit the seed rails and hang it
steps.body:
Measure the assembled feeder's platform length, cut the rail stock to fit, rip into two 1-1/2" strips, and glue and nail one along each open side. The rails keep seed on the platform and double as perches.

Add the eye hook at the peak (pilot it — 1/2" cedar splits), hang it in view of a window, and leave the cedar raw: it weathers to silver and the birds could not care less.
materials: Exterior wood glue, rated for oily woods

images.alt: Small gabled cedar bird feeder with open seed platform and side perch rails
